claim.hypotheses_location: Pages 751–753: Text introducing the general causal model and explicitly listing H1–H4 (e.g., 'The two preliminary hypotheses in this study… H1… H2…' and later 'H3… H4…'); associated with Fig. 1 and Fig. 2.

data.source: Self-report questionnaires administered to Israel Defense Forces (IDF) soldiers during basic training
data.sample_size: 286 male soldiers (armored corps n=169; infantry n=117); analytic samples for SEM ranged from N=236 to N=238 due to missing data
data.notes: Participants volunteered and anonymity was assured. Response rate: armored 65%, infantry 55%. Between 10–20% new immigrants with language difficulties were excluded. Measures included adult attachment (anxiety and avoidance; security computed as low anxiety and avoidance), trait anxiety (STAI), openness to experiences (abbreviated 24-item OE, validated against NEO-PI-R), and leadership-shaping experiences (7-item LSE). Items were parceled into three indicators per construct for SEM. Hebrew versions used; some items reverse-scored. A pilot study preceded the main data collection.

method.description: Cross-sectional survey of IDF recruits using validated questionnaires to assess adult attachment tendencies, trait anxiety, openness to experience, and leadership-shaping experiences from childhood to conscription. Constructs were modeled via parceling and analyzed with SEM (AMOS 5) to test a hypothesized developmental pathway from secure attachment to lower trait anxiety and higher openness, which in turn predict greater leadership-related experiences. Multiple alternative structural models were also tested and compared using standard fit indices.
steps: Recruit a representative sample of male IDF soldiers nearing completion of basic training; obtain voluntary participation and assure anonymity.

Administer self-report questionnaires: adult attachment (anxiety and avoidance items; security inferred as low anxiety/avoidance), trait anxiety (STAI), openness to experiences (abbreviated 24-item OE), and leadership-shaping experiences (7 Likert items).
Score scales (including reverse scoring where applicable); compute reliability statistics.
Parcel items randomly into three indicators per latent construct to reduce error and simplify the measurement model.
Estimate the measurement model (CFA) to confirm latent constructs and indicators.

Estimate the hypothesized structural model (Model 1: secure attachment → trait anxiety (−), openness (+); trait anxiety (−) and openness (+) → leadership experiences) using AMOS 5; report fit indices.
Estimate alternative structural models (Models 2–6), compare using chi-square difference tests when nested and ECVI/fit indices otherwise.
Interpret path coefficients and variance explained for leadership experiences.
method.models: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with confirmatory factor analysis (measurement model) and multiple structural models (Models 1–6) assessing paths among secure attachment, trait anxiety, openness to experiences, and leadership experiences.
method.outcome_variable: Leadership experiences (Leadership-Shaping Experiences; 7-item Likert composite reflecting responsibility and influence roles at home, school, and social settings prior to military service)
method.independent_variables: Secure attachment style (low anxiety and avoidance), trait anxiety (STAI), openness to experiences (24-item OE scale)

results.summary: The hypothesized SEM (Model 1) fit the data well and supported all four hypotheses. Secure attachment strongly and negatively predicted trait anxiety (β = −0.62, p < .01) and positively predicted openness to experience (β = 0.24, p < .01). Trait anxiety negatively predicted leadership experiences (β = −0.23, p < .01), while openness to experience positively predicted leadership experiences (β = 0.34, p < .01). The model explained 19% of the variance in leadership experiences. Adding a direct path from secure attachment to leadership experiences (Model 2) did not yield a significant effect (β = 0.18, p = .12) and reduced the significance of the trait anxiety path. A model with all three predictors directly predicting leadership (Model 3) fit poorly. A model positioning anxiety as the main exogenous variable (Model 4) fit acceptably, but its direct path to leadership experiences was not significant. A model with openness as the exogenous driver (Model 5) fit poorly. A final stable model (Model 6) treated secure attachment and trait anxiety as correlated exogenous predictors of openness and leadership experiences.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Leadership experiences (predicted by openness to experience)
Leadership experiences (direct path from secure attachment; Model 2)

metadata.original_paper_title: Attachment and leader's development via experiences
